turns.text: Hello Emiko, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I’m happy to help, though talking about it… it’s a bit strange. I spend my days telling other people how to nourish their bodies, how to manage their macros, how to stay healthy. Now, looking at this bowl of spinach, I just feel like a fraud. My stomach is this constant, roiling knot, and the irony isn't lost on me.

It’s hard, honestly. When I go into the clinic, I see that look they get—the "advanced maternal age" look. They see the numbers on the chart, they see the "medium risk" label, and they start ticking boxes. I find myself nodding along, playing the role of the dietitian, the professional. I think I do it because if I act like I have it all together, maybe they won't look too closely at how much I'm shaking inside. 

Every time I’m on that exam table, the room feels too small, and the silence… it feels heavy. I had a loss before this, and no one really knows. I haven't told my coworkers, and with my husband working so much, I’m mostly just here, waiting for the bus, worrying about every little twinge. It’s hard to feel like a "patient" when all you really feel is terrified. Does that make sense?
Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

It wasn’t a sudden epiphany. At forty-three, I think I’d spent so many years carefully building a life that felt safe and predictable that the idea of a child felt like a beautiful, distant concept—something I’d missed the train for. My career as a dietitian gave me a sense of control, which, given my history, was something I really craved. But then, there was this shift. A quiet, persistent ache that didn't go away.

When I finally got that positive test, the feeling wasn’t just "joy." It was an immediate, visceral sense of vulnerability. It felt like I was stepping onto thin ice after spending decades walking on solid ground. 

I remember sitting in my kitchen, staring at the little plastic stick, and instead of calling someone, I just went quiet. I felt like if I breathed too hard, I’d break the spell. There was so much joy, yes, but it was anchored to the ground by a heavy, cold fear—the memory of my last loss. It felt like standing on a cliff edge. I wanted to tell the world, but the silence felt like a protective shell. I was terrified that if I spoke it out loud, the universe might decide to take it back. It’s been a very solitary, quiet beginning to something that’s supposed to be celebrated.
Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

As a dietitian, I’m supposed to have the perfect roadmap, right? I spent months—maybe even years, if I’m honest—optimizing everything. I was obsessive about my folate levels, tracking my glucose like it was a religion, and making sure my body was essentially a pristine vessel. I thought if I just did everything "right"—if I followed the science to the letter—I could earn a healthy pregnancy. 

But there’s a cruel distance between what you read in a textbook and what happens in your body. I thought I was preparing for a baby, but I think I was really just trying to build a fortress against another loss. I wanted to control the outcome because, deep down, I know how easily things can slip away. 

The hardest part of that preparation wasn't the vitamins or the diet; it was trying to prepare my mind. I read the articles about age and risk, and every time I saw a statistic, I felt like I was reading my own obituary. No amount of nutrient-dense greens prepares you for the moment you realize you can’t control the heartbeat. I did everything I was *supposed* to do, but I still feel like I'm holding my breath, waiting for the other shoe to drop. It turns out, you can’t meal-plan your way out of anxiety.
Where did you go to find out what you needed to know — friends, family, online, a doctor?

That’s the thing—I didn't really go to anyone. My life feels like this island I’ve built to keep the past at bay. My husband is wonderful, but he's exhausted from his shifts, and I can't bring myself to add the weight of my panic to his shoulders. I don't have friends nearby who I feel comfortable "unloading" on, and honestly, the thought of talking to someone who might offer platitudes like, "everything happens for a reason," makes me want to scream.

So, I turned to the only thing I trusted: clinical data. I spent hours reading journals, analyzing studies on maternal age, and obsessing over clinical outcomes. But even that backfired. Reading about the risks just turned my anxiety into something academic and clinical. It’s like, I know the statistics of my own body better than I know how to ask for help.

When I finally went to the doctor, I felt like I was presenting a case study rather than a person. I didn't want to admit I was scared, so I spoke the language of the clinic—testing schedules, nutrient requirements, protocols. I think I did that because if I acted like the professional, maybe they’d see me as a partner in the process instead of just another "advanced maternal age" case to be managed. It’s a lonely way to prepare. I’m drowning in information, but I’m starving for someone to just ask me how I *am*.
Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

I didn't have many, honestly. Because of my history, I was terrified that if I walked into a clinic *before* I was pregnant, they’d look at my file, see my age and my past, and tell me it was a mistake to even try. It sounds irrational, I know, but that’s the shadow I live in. I felt like I had to prove my body could hold a pregnancy before I dared to ask for professional help.

When I finally did go in, it wasn't a conversation—it was an assessment. It felt like I was being processed. I sat there in the paper gown, listening to them talk about "maternal age" as if it were a synonym for "catastrophe." Every time they mentioned my risk level, I felt smaller. I wanted to talk about my fears, about the trauma that makes a pelvic exam feel like a violation, but every time I opened my mouth, the dietitian in me took over. I’d start asking about screening protocols or vitamin absorption rates, just to keep the conversation on safe, clinical ground. 

I think they saw a high-functioning patient who had it all under control. They didn't see the woman who had to pull over on the bus ride home because she was shaking so hard she couldn't breathe. I desperately wanted them to stop the slideshow of risks and just look at me, but I didn't know how to ask for that without feeling like I was failing at being a "good" patient.
Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

It was a season of quiet, frantic arithmetic. Every month felt like a high-stakes exam I hadn't studied for, even though I knew every variable. There’s something profoundly lonely about tracking ovulation and watching for symptoms when you’re carrying the secret of a previous loss. I treated my own body like a chemistry experiment, adjusting my intake, monitoring every shift, trying to eliminate every possible variable that might lead to a repeat of the past.

What stands out most isn't the science; it’s the silence. My husband and I were both working so much that our "intimacy" started to feel like a scheduled meeting. We were both terrified, I think, but we were both too afraid to break the silence and admit that we were scared to hope. I remember standing in our kitchen after another negative test, and the silence in the room was so loud it felt like it was ringing in my ears. I felt like a failure—not just as a woman, but as a professional who should somehow be able to "fix" this. I felt like my body was a traitor. 

Every negative was a reminder of what I’d already lost, and every positive felt like a fragile, terrifying promise I wasn't sure I could keep. It was a time of holding my breath, waiting for a life I wasn't sure was meant for me.
Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

I think my idea of pregnancy was mostly formed by the textbooks I studied and the patients I’ve counseled. I had this pristine, clinical image in my head: the glowing mother, the meticulously managed nutrient intake, the steady progression of growth. I saw it as a professional project—something to be optimized and monitored for a perfect outcome. I think I wanted to believe that if I was disciplined enough, I could bypass the messiness of biology.

But deep down, there was a different, more haunting image. It came from the shadows of my own history. I’ve always pictured motherhood as a place where you are constantly standing guard, watching for threats, and feeling responsible for every heartbeat. It’s not the serene, soft-focus imagery you see in magazines; it’s a vigilant, almost military-style watchfulness. 

Because I’ve felt so lost and disconnected, I think I’ve been trying to "mother" myself into being a mother. I’ve been trying to fill the gaps in my support system with information. But none of those books or charts ever mentioned the nausea that feels like a weight, or the exhaustion of sitting on a cold bus while worrying if you’re doing it right. My vision of being a parent is a mix of clinical precision and a desperate, aching need to finally be someone’s safe harbor.
How much were those ideas shaped by your own family's experiences — your mother's, or others close to you?

My mother… we don’t talk about these things. Growing up, there was a kind of unspoken stoicism in our house. You didn’t complain, you didn't air your grievances, and you certainly didn’t show weakness. You just got on with the work. I think that’s where I learned to hide my own fears behind a wall of "doing." If I’m busy, if I’m researching, if I’m being the expert, then I’m not being the frightened daughter.

But there’s a distance there, too. I don't have a model for how to be vulnerable with a pregnancy. My mother’s path wasn't anything I could emulate, and honestly, the thought of trying to navigate this while being watched by family feels like adding more pressure to a system that’s already redlining. I feel like I’m building this experience from scratch, which is probably why I’m so anxious. I don’t have a script for how to be held or supported because that wasn't really a feature of my upbringing.

I look at other women sometimes, the ones who seem to have this village around them, and it feels like a different language. I’m just trying to keep my own heart beating in time with this little one’s, and doing it all in this quiet, solitary way I’ve perfected. It’s like I’m trying to rewrite the story, but I don't know the ending yet.
Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

The reality of being forty-three and starting this journey is that the practicalities aren't just details—they're constant, grinding stressors. I work full-time, and while that provides structure, it also leaves me with almost no margin for the reality of early pregnancy. My husband is working even harder, picking up extra shifts to prepare for the future, but it means we’re like ships passing in the night. We're "prepared" on paper, but we’re emotionally depleted.

Then there’s the sheer logistics. I don’t have a car. You wouldn't think that would be a defining part of a pregnancy story, but it is. My world is narrowed down to the bus schedule. When I’m nauseous, when my body is aching, when I’m already carrying that knot of fear from my history—having to stand at a bus stop in the cold for forty minutes to get to a routine appointment feels like a monumental task. I arrive at the clinic already exhausted, my clothes damp, my heart racing from the stress of the commute, and then I’m expected to sit in a waiting room and be the "perfect, informed patient." 

It feels like my life is being managed by spreadsheets, but nobody is accounting for the cost of just showing up. I’m exhausted, I’m isolated, and I’m terrified—and that doesn't fit on any of my charts.
Were there things you felt you needed to have 'in place' before starting, and did that match what actually happened?

I had a checklist, of course. I wanted my finances settled, my career at a place where I could breathe, and most importantly, I wanted to feel "ready"—whatever that myth actually means. I wanted to be that woman who could handle the uncertainty with grace. I thought if I had the right job, the right insurance, and enough knowledge, I could create a safe environment where nothing bad could happen.

But it didn’t match reality at all. You can’t "prepare" for the way the world tilts when you’re pregnant and suddenly everything is unpredictable. I have the job and the insurance, but I don't have the peace of mind I thought would come with them. 

The biggest gap between the plan and the reality is the isolation. I thought "having things in place" meant logistical boxes checked. I never realized that what I *actually* needed was a hand to hold when I'm sitting on that cold bus, or someone to say, "It’s okay to be scared," without trying to fix my blood levels or lecture me on maternal age. I’m doing this "right" by every metric, yet I’ve never felt more adrift. I built the infrastructure, but I forgot to build the sanctuary. Now, I’m just trying to keep my head above water while everyone thinks I’m sailing perfectly.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

The moment I saw that second line, I didn't feel a rush of euphoria. I felt a cold, sharp drop in my stomach, like I’d suddenly lost my footing on a steep staircase. I remember dropping the stick into the trash and just staring at the bathroom tile. I didn't call my husband. I didn't cry tears of joy. I just stood there, waiting for the "thump" of my own heart to calm down so I could figure out what to do next. It felt like I was holding a live bird in my hands—something so fragile and precious that I was terrified I’d accidentally crush it.

The weeks that followed have been… gray. That’s the only way to describe it. The nausea isn't just a physical thing; it’s a constant, nagging reminder that my body is under siege. I go to work, I talk to clients about their health, and all the while, I’m listening to my own body for signs of betrayal. Every time I go to the bathroom, I’m terrified of what I’ll see. It’s like living with a ghost. I haven't told a soul. I keep tucking my feelings away, deeper and deeper, just to keep the "normal" mask firmly in place. I’m carrying this massive, heavy secret, and the weight of it is starting to make it hard to breathe.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

It’s been like performing a play where I’m the only one who knows the script is a tragedy. Every appointment is a blur of cold stirrups and numbers. They see the chart, they see the age, and they immediately start talking about "advanced maternal age" and "risks." I try to be the helpful, knowledgeable colleague, using clinical terms to mask the fact that I’m actually trembling. 

The worst part is the disconnect. There’s a specific, clinical chill to the way they handle me. Because I’m a dietitian, they seem to assume I don’t need the "human" touch. They talk at me about nutrition and monitoring, but they never ask me, "How are you holding up?" 

During one exam, I felt that familiar, paralyzing panic—the kind that comes from my past—and I just went quiet. I dissociated. I’m sure they just thought I was a stoic, professional woman who didn't need much hand-holding. They don't know that every time they touch me, I’m mentally fighting to stay in the room. I feel like a biological event they’re managing, not a person navigating a miracle and a nightmare all at once. I leave those appointments feeling more invisible than when I arrived. It’s supposed to be a journey, but it just feels like being processed.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

The screening process was the most clinical, cold part of the whole experience. Because of my age, it felt like the options were laid out with a sort of inevitable gravity. They handed me brochures with charts about chromosomal risks, and again, my own professional background betrayed me. I looked at those numbers not as a patient, but as a dietitian—I started calculating the percentages, looking at the standard deviations, trying to use logic to mitigate the fear. 

But there’s no logic that can quiet the voice in the back of your head that remembers loss. I remember sitting there, clutching those papers, feeling like I was being asked to decide on the fate of my own happiness based on a series of probabilities. 

Deciding to go through with the testing felt less like a choice and more like a surrender to the system. I did it because that’s what "good patients" at forty-three do—we test, we monitor, we manage. I didn't ask the questions I actually wanted to ask, like "What happens if this doesn't go well?" or "Can you just hold my hand while we wait for the results?" I just agreed to the protocol. I felt like if I questioned the process, I was somehow failing the pregnancy. It’s all so transactional. I traded my peace of mind for data, and honestly, the data hasn't made me feel any safer. It just gave me more numbers to obsess over when I’m lying awake at night.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

I honestly wish I could say I turned to a supportive friend or a community, but the truth is, I’ve mostly just turned inward, which is probably the most dangerous place I could be. I’ve been relying on clinical journals and the same resources I’d recommend to my own patients. It feels safe because it’s objective. In those pages, the pregnancy isn't a person with a heartbeat that might stop; it’s a series of nutrient requirements, hormonal shifts, and risk factors. Dealing with the science is how I try to keep the fear from swallowing me whole.

But it’s a hollow kind of support. When I’m sitting on the bus, staring at the floor, those clinical facts don't offer any comfort. They don't make the nausea better, and they don't replace the feeling of a human being saying, "I know you're terrified, and it's okay." 

I feel like a fraud. I'm a professional who is supposed to have the map, but I’m navigating a wilderness I wasn't prepared for. Every time I search for information, I find data, not connection. I’ve been trying to "dietitian" my way through a human experience, and I’m realizing that no amount of research can prepare you for the raw, aching reality of hoping for a child when you’re already grieving the one you haven't even met yet.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

It’s a complicated, almost surreal distance. I’ve spent my entire career analyzing body composition, macronutrients, and metabolic function. I know exactly what’s happening in my uterus at a cellular level, but that clinical precision doesn't make it feel any more like *my* body. It feels like a borrowed machine that’s operating on its own, increasingly unpredictable rhythm. 

Physically, the nausea is the most aggressive part. It’s this gray, persistent hum that makes my own expertise feel like a taunt. I tell patients to prioritize leafy greens and steady blood glucose, yet the sight of spinach makes me want to weep. It feels like my body is rejecting the very "perfect patient" script I’ve written for it. 

Emotionally? I feel like I’m in a state of suspended animation. I don't dare feel too attached to the changes, because I’m so terrified of the empty silence that might follow. When I see a slight shift in my shape in the mirror, I don't see "pregnancy glow"—I see a countdown. Every day that passes is one day closer to the unknown. I find myself holding my breath, treating my own belly like something fragile that doesn't fully belong to me yet. It’s like being a guest in my own skin, watching the changes and praying that I’m allowed to keep them.
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

It’s the loss of control, definitely. My entire professional identity is built on the idea that if you understand the systems—the metabolism, the digestion, the hormonal responses—you can master them. I’ve spent my life convinced that "health" is a result of my own effort. But pregnancy is a brute-force violation of that logic.

The nausea is particularly cruel. It’s not just a symptom; it feels like my body is actively rebelling against my own expertise. I feel like such a hypocrite, sitting in my clinic office advising a patient on balanced meals when I can barely swallow a cracker without my stomach turning. It makes me feel like a failure, and I hate that. 

Then there’s the exhaustion. It’s this deep, marrow-level tiredness that no amount of sleep seems to touch. I’ve always been someone who prides herself on efficiency, on being the one who gets things done. Now, I have to stop and catch my breath after walking a few blocks to the bus stop, and that’s hard to come to terms with. I feel… fragile. And in my world, being "fragile" is the same thing as being broken. I’m having to learn how to exist in a body that isn't under my total command, and that is a terrifying place to be.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

My husband, he’s… well, he’s trying in his own exhausted way. He’s working those long shifts, and I know he’s doing it for us—for the future he hopes we’re building. But the weight of the silence between us is heavy. When he’s home, he’s so drained, and I’m so guarded. I think we’re both scared to name the fear out loud. If we talk about the baby, it makes the possibility of losing it feel realer, and neither of us has the heart to go there again. So, we dance around it. We talk about the schedule, the bills, the logistics, but we never talk about the *terror*.

As for family or friends? I keep them at arm’s length. I don't want the pity or the "congratulations" until I’m absolutely sure this is going to stick. I feel like if I invite people in, I’m just giving them a front-row seat to my potential failure. It’s easier to be isolated. It’s safer to be the professional who has it all under control than the woman who’s barely holding on to the edge of the cliff. It’s a lonely way to grow a life, but sometimes it feels like the only way I know how to survive.
How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?

He’s never really been there, and in a way, I’ve made sure of it. Between his long, unpredictable shifts and my own fierce need to maintain this "professional" mask, it’s almost become a relief that he’s not in those exam rooms. It sounds awful to say, I know. But if he were sitting there, watching the doctor look at me with that "advanced maternal age" pity, I think the sheer weight of his worry—or worse, his forced optimism—would break me. 

I’ve kept the clinical space for myself. It’s my battleground. I go in, I recite the numbers, I listen to the heartbeat, and I leave. If he were there, I’d have to perform *for two people*. I’d have to be the strong, capable partner, the woman who knows the science, the woman who isn't terrified of the heartbeat stopping. Keeping him away feels like a way to protect him from the reality of the anxiety I’m carrying. 

But then I walk out of the clinic alone, waiting for that bus in the cold, and the isolation hits me like a physical blow. I’m doing this whole thing as a solo project, and I think that’s exactly how I’ve been conditioned to handle trauma. It’s not fair to him, and it’s not fair to me, but I don’t know how else to be.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

The fear is a physical weight, like a cold stone sitting right in the center of my chest. It never leaves. Most of it is just the sheer, terrifying statistics of "advanced maternal age." I know them by heart. I know the risks of miscarriage, the risks of complications—I know all the things that can go wrong because I’ve spent my life studying them. It’s a curse, really, knowing too much. It turns every twinge, every cramp, every moment of fatigue into a potential warning sign.

But there’s a deeper, darker fear, too—the one I don’t talk about. I’m scared that if I get too close to this, if I let myself truly believe that this baby is going to stay, the universe will snatch it away just to prove how little control I actually have. My history of loss isn't just a medical note; it’s a phantom that walks beside me everywhere I go. 

I worry about the birth, too. Not the pain, but the vulnerability. Being in a hospital, being examined, being "cared for" by people who see my chart instead of me—it feels like walking into a trap. I feel like I have to be perfect, or else they’ll decide I’m not capable of doing this. It’s a constant, suffocating pressure to be the "good patient," even while I’m barely keeping my head above water.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

I haven’t. I don’t think I could even if I wanted to. Whenever I sit across from them, the dietitian in me takes the wheel. I’ll start asking them about my glucose levels, or if there’s any new research on prenatal supplement absorption—I treat it like a clinical consultation. It’s a defense mechanism, I suppose. 

If I told them that my chest feels tight every time I wait for a heartbeat scan, or that I’m having flashbacks during the pelvic exams because of past trauma, they’d look at me with that professional, clinical sympathy. And that would break me. I don’t want their pity, and I certainly don't want to be labeled as "anxious" or "high-risk" in a way that goes beyond the physical. 

I’m terrified that if I admit how scared I actually am, they’ll see me as unstable. I’ve worked so hard to maintain this image of the "perfect patient"—the one who follows all the protocols and doesn't make a fuss—that I’ve essentially barred myself from getting the emotional help I desperately need. They look at my chart, see my age, and see a medical problem to be managed. They don’t see a woman who is trying to survive the quiet terror of being pregnant again. So, I stay silent. I keep the fear to myself, like a stone I’m carrying in my pocket, and I just nod and agree.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I wanted a partnership. I think I dreamed of someone who would look past the "advanced maternal age" tag and actually see me—a human being who was coming to this experience with a history of trauma and loss. I wanted someone who would say, "I know you’re terrified, and that’s a valid way to feel," rather than just reciting the standard deviation risks of my chromosomal screening. I hoped for a space where I could exhale, where I could be the patient instead of having to be the professional.

But deep down, I was always worried it would become what it is now: a transactional, clinical assessment. I feared they would treat my body like a research project, focusing on the data points while ignoring the woman shaking on the exam table. I was worried they would mistake my knowledge for a lack of fear. Because I know the science, I think they assume I don’t need the compassion, or maybe they just don't have the time to offer it. 

The reality is that I’m not a partner in my care; I’m a case study. I feel like I have to keep my guard up, performing this "perfect patient" role just to maintain some semblance of dignity. It’s exactly the dynamic I feared, but I’ve played my part in it too well, I suppose.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

They weren’t really "conversations" in the way I think you mean. There was no sitting down with a cup of tea to map out our future. My life has been such a carefully calibrated, solitary effort that when we finally talked about it, it felt like we were tiptoeing around a sleeping beast. We’re both so tired from our jobs, and we’ve been living in this reactive mode for so long, that the decision felt like something that just sort of... happened to us.

I think I held the most say, but it wasn't a power play. It was more that I felt the clock ticking in my bones, and I was the one who had to manage the risks and the history of my own body. He supported me, of course, but I felt this immense, suffocating responsibility to be the one to "make it work." I researched the fertility windows, I tracked the cycles, I prepared the physical space. I took on the burden of the "project."

Did it feel right? It felt necessary, but it was incredibly lonely. I carried the weight of the decision like I carried the weight of everything else—alone. I didn't want to drag him into my anxiety, so I kept the most difficult parts of the decision-making to myself. Looking back, I realize I built a cage around my own choice, and now I’m the one stuck inside it.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

My mother has always been a woman of iron-willed silence. She never explicitly said, "You should have a baby," but her silence—and the way she looked at me during family gatherings—carried a weight that felt like an unspoken mandate. It was this constant, heavy expectation that I would eventually fulfill the role of "mother," even if she never offered the warmth that would make me feel capable of it.

That pressure from her, that old-fashioned sense of what a woman’s life *ought* to look like, seeped into my own mind. Even though I’ve built this professional life for myself, a part of me always felt like I was somehow incomplete without a child. I felt like I was failing a test that I didn’t even sign up for.

It's strange, but I think I’ve been trying to prove to her—and to myself—that I could handle this "life project" perfectly. I wanted to show that I could be the woman she expected me to be, but without the messiness, without the vulnerability, and without the need for support. It’s like I’m trying to check a box in my own personal ledger, just to prove I can. But that realization doesn't make the fear any smaller. It just makes me feel like I’m fulfilling a duty rather than living a life.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before I was pregnant, I viewed my body as a machine to be tuned. I was the dietitian, the one in control—I thought if I put in the right input, I’d get the right output. I’ve always been proud of that discipline. It made me feel safe. I didn't see my body as a source of life; I saw it as a set of systems I could master.

But the moment the possibility of pregnancy arrived, my perspective shifted from pride to suspicion. It felt like my body was suddenly a fickle, untrustworthy vessel. After my previous loss, I stopped looking at it as a machine I could master and started seeing it as a traitor. I became obsessed with "fixing" it, as if my biology was a series of errors I needed to correct.

Now, as I’m in this first trimester, I feel like I’m constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop. I don't feel capable; I feel fragile. Every time I get nauseous or feel a twinge, I don't think, "My body is doing its job." I think, "Here it goes again—something is failing." I’ve lost that sense of ownership I used to have, and in its place is this hollow, aching vulnerability. I’m just trying to hold it together, but I feel like I’m living in a stranger's body, one that might decide to let go at any moment.
Were there things about your body you felt confident about, and things that worried you or felt uncertain?

I used to be so confident in my body’s resilience, specifically its ability to withstand stress. As a dietitian working long hours, I took pride in being able to power through hunger, fatigue, and the sheer pace of a busy clinical day. My body felt like a tool I’d honed to perfection—efficient, reliable, and entirely under my control. I trusted it because I maintained it so rigorously.

But that confidence evaporated the second I saw the positive test. The very things I once thought were strengths now feel like liabilities. That "powering through" mentality? It just masks the symptoms I should probably be listening to. And my age... I can’t escape that. It’s the one variable I couldn't optimize away with a clean diet or a supplement regimen. It’s like a ceiling hanging over me.

Now, everything feels uncertain. I’m terrified of the internal, silent things—the hormone levels I can’t track by eye, the placental development, the heartbeat that I worry will just go quiet again. My confidence has been replaced by this constant, low-grade hyper-vigilance. I look at my reflection and I don’t see a capable woman; I see someone holding her breath, waiting to see if her own body will betray her one more time. It’s a very lonely, fragile kind of uncertainty.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

I remember going in for a routine physical about a year before I conceived. The doctor was pleasant enough, but when the topic of my age came up—just in passing, as part of the standard history review—the atmosphere in the room shifted. It became sterile, almost dismissive. She pulled up the charts, pointed out the risks, and gave me that look—the one that says, *“You’re playing with fire, aren’t you?”* 

It felt less like a conversation and more like a warning. She didn't ask if I had support or how I was feeling about the possibility of pregnancy; she just gave me the statistics on decreased fertility and higher-risk outcomes. I walked out of that office feeling like my biological clock wasn't just a metaphor, but a deadline I was actively failing to meet. 

It absolutely shaped my expectations for maternity care. I walked away convinced that if I ever did get pregnant, I’d be treated as a medical liability rather than a person. That encounter taught me that the system doesn't have room for my humanity—it only has space for my risk factors. That’s why I decided to be "perfect" when I finally did get pregnant; I felt like I had to be beyond reproach so they wouldn't have any reason to treat me like a problem to be solved.
Did you see the same person more than once, or were you always meeting someone new?

It’s been a revolving door. I don’t think I’ve seen the same face twice, really. One week it’s a nurse practitioner for the blood draw, the next it’s a different doctor for the scan, then someone else entirely for a routine check-in. It feels like I’m constantly re-introducing myself to a fresh set of eyes that immediately scan my chart for the "advanced maternal age" flag. 

There’s no continuity, no sense that anyone actually knows my story—or that they’d even want to. Every time I walk in, I have to perform the same "everything is fine" routine. I have to briefly summarize my history, nod through their practiced, generic advice, and hope they don't ask a question that forces me to drop the mask. 

It makes the care feel so... transactional. Like I’m just a series of appointments being checked off a list. I wanted a guide for this, someone I could actually trust to see the person behind the diagnosis, but instead, I’m just navigating a sea of strangers. It’s hard to feel cared for when you’re just another file on a desk that they’ve only glanced at for thirty seconds before walking into the room. I’m starting to wonder if "continuity of care" is just something they write in the brochures.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

Conversations? There weren't many. My circle is so small, and I’ve spent so long guarding my privacy that I’ve almost forgotten how to open up. My husband and I—we’d touch on it, but it was always from a distance, like we were debating a hypothetical future rather than our actual, tangible lives. We’d talk about the "what-ifs"—the logistics of daycare, the career shifts, the sheer exhaustion—but never the *fear*. I don’t think either of us wanted to break the other person's heart by admitting how much we were both terrified of history repeating itself.

I didn't have friends I could talk to, really. I felt like if I voiced my desire, I’d be inviting scrutiny I wasn't ready to handle. Would people think I was too old? Too set in my ways? It’s easier to just do it in silence.

Honestly, the "input" that mattered most wasn't even from a person. It was the data. I spent so much time reading clinical studies, analyzing trends, and treating my life like a high-stakes research project. In a way, I trusted those nameless authors more than I trusted anyone I knew, because they dealt in cold, hard facts rather than well-meaning platitudes. It’s a miserable way to live, but at the time, it felt like the only way to keep my feet on the ground.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

It was always the weight of my own past. Before I ever felt a kick or dealt with morning sickness, I felt the phantom of my previous loss like a physical pressure against my ribs. I’ve always been someone who demands excellence from myself, and I suppose I projected that onto my biology. I viewed my body through such a critical, analytical lens that it stopped feeling like a home and started feeling like a malfunctioning piece of equipment.

I remember thinking, *What if I’m not built for this?* Not just in a physiological sense—my age, my history—but in an emotional one. I had this nagging suspicion that I wasn't "soft" enough, or "open" enough, to actually nurture something. I’ve spent so much of my life being guarded, being the expert, being the person who doesn't need help. How do you go from being an island to being a harbor for a child? 

It felt like I was trying to force a square peg into a round hole. I was confident in my mind, in my career, in my clinical knowledge, but my own body felt like a volatile variable I couldn't account for. The uncertainty wasn't just about the biology; it was about whether I was capable of surrendering the control I’d spent forty years perfecting. It was, and still is, a terrifying shift.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

There wasn't one. That’s the most honest answer I can give. 

I think I convinced myself that I didn’t *need* that person because, professionally, I’m supposed to be the one giving the support. I spend my days being the reliable resource for other women, helping them navigate their own health, so asking for that same kind of guidance for myself felt like a professional failure. It felt like I was admitting I wasn't the "expert" of my own life.

My husband is a wonderful, steady man, but he’s not a navigator in this space. He’s in the trenches with me, but he’s just as lost as I am. We were both just doing the best we could, which mostly meant keeping our heads down and pushing forward. 

So, I turned to the literature. I treated my own body like a patient I was managing. If I had a question, I’d look up a journal, I’d cross-reference the data, and I’d formulate my own answer. It was a cold, efficient way to manage a life-changing event, but it was the only way I knew how to stay "safe." Looking back, it’s heartbreaking that I felt I couldn't lean on anyone. I built this entire experience on a foundation of solitude, and now that the stakes are this high, the loneliness is almost as heavy as the pregnancy itself.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

It’s a complicated answer. On one hand, yes, I made the choice. My husband and I talked about the logistics, the finances, the career paths. But there’s this crushing weight of biology that doesn't care about your timeline. At forty-three, "your own time" is a luxury you don't really have. It felt less like a leisurely choice and more like a sprint against a deadline that was ticking down in my blood.

And then there's the internal pressure—the expectation that I’d "complete" my life. I think I felt a duty to prove I could do this, almost like I was correcting an omission in my own history. It wasn't just my choice; it felt like a response to all those years I’d spent building a "perfect" life that still felt empty. 

I don't think I ever felt truly free, because the shadow of my previous loss was always there, dictating the terms. It felt like I was trying to reclaim something, but the cost of that pursuit was this constant, anxious surveillance of my own body. It was my choice, but it was a choice made in a state of high alert, surrounded by risks and statistics that felt like they were holding me hostage. Does that make sense? It feels like I walked through a door I chose to open, but I didn't realize how much of myself I’d have to leave behind to fit through it.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

It was never just about what I *wanted*; it was always governed by the tyranny of my age. At forty-three, the window doesn't just close; it slams shut. That felt less like a choice and more like a high-stakes ultimatum from my own biology. I felt like I was being forced to choose between the safety of my controlled, professional life and the terrifying, uncertain leap into motherhood, and the pressure of knowing I didn't have years to weigh it was suffocating.

Financially, we’re in a place where we *can* afford a child, but the cost of the care—the extra monitoring, the specialist appointments, the logistics of transport—it’s like a hidden tax on "advanced maternal age." And then there’s the cultural expectation. People look at a woman my age and they see either "selfish" or "struggling." I felt like I had to prove I was the exception.

My husband and I are partners, but the weight of the biological clock sits squarely on my shoulders. I couldn’t ask him to wait, and I couldn't wait myself. It felt like I was standing in the middle of an intersection, watching the light turn yellow, and knowing if I didn't cross *right now*, I’d never get another chance. That’s not a choice; that’s a desperation masked as a decision.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

It’s an illusion of control, honestly. I thought I was in the driver’s seat because I’m the one who researched every protocol, questioned every screening, and maintained my own health stats. But the moment I step into that clinic, I realize I’m just a passenger in a system that doesn’t care about my map. They have their own route, their own pace, and their own rigid set of rules for "advanced maternal age." 

I hand the wheel over every single time I walk through those doors. I do it because I’m terrified that if I try to take control—if I start demanding things or questioning their "standard of care"—I’ll be labeled as "difficult" or "uncooperative." And in the hierarchy of the clinic, you don't want to be the difficult patient. So, I smile, I nod, and I let them take the lead, even when it feels like they’re driving us toward a destination I didn’t choose.

It’s exhausting to perform this compliance. I feel like a professional collaborator who’s actually being sidelined. I’m waiting for the moment where I can truly advocate for myself, but the fear of being seen as "unstable" or "high-risk" keeps me quiet. I’m in the car, yes, but I’m definitely not driving. I’m just holding on, hoping they know the way, even when the silence in the room tells me they don’t see me at all.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

It’s hard to be on the same page when we’re both reading from different books. My husband, he’s terrified of the loss we’ve already had, so he tends to push for whatever the doctors say. To him, the medical system is the only thing standing between us and catastrophe. If the clinic suggests a test, he sees safety. I, on the other hand, see the bureaucracy of "advanced maternal age" and the cold, clinical distance. I see the lack of humanity.

We don't really "navigate" those decisions together; we sort of stumble into them. I do the deep dives—I look at the medical journals and the success rates—and I present him with the "logical" choice, mostly to spare him from having to see the raw, exposed nerves of my anxiety. He trusts me to make the call because he knows I’m the professional. That hurts, in a way. I’m carrying the burden of being the expert *and* the expectant mother, and sometimes I just want to be able to turn to him and say, "I have no idea what to do, and I'm scared." 

Instead, we end up in this silent agreement where I decide, he nods, and we both just hope we’re doing it right. We’re aligned in our fear, but we’re not aligned in how we face it. I think he thinks I’m managing it, and I think he’s managing, but we’re both just drifting along, keeping our own versions of the truth to ourselves.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

It’s a strange balance. On the purely logistical things—the finances, the insurance paperwork, the "official" decisions like which hospital to register at—his input is essential. I need to know he's on board with the path we're taking, because he’s my only bridge to the outside world. When we discuss those things, it feels like we’re a team. That’s when I feel like a wife, not just a "high-risk patient."

But when it comes to the internal, visceral decisions—like the screening tests, or how I handle the fear, or the specific way I respond to the medical team—that has to be my call alone. It’s not that I don’t value his opinion; it’s that his perspective is too anchored in hope. He wants so badly for this to work that he’ll agree to anything the doctors suggest because he thinks it’s the price of a healthy baby. I can’t afford that kind of blind faith. I have to look at the cold reality of my history, my body, and my trauma.

When I’m in that room, it’s my body on the table. It’s my anxiety that’s being triggered by the pelvic exams. It’s my heart that stops every time they mention "advanced maternal age." I need to be the one who calls those shots because I’m the one who has to live with the fallout, regardless of the outcome. It feels selfish, maybe, but it’s the only way I can protect the sliver of myself that hasn’t been consumed by the clinic.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the thing that hurts the most, and that I haven't quite been able to put into words, is how much I feel like I’ve lost my own identity in this "pregnancy project." I’m a professional, I’m a dietitian, I’m a woman who has spent decades building a life I’m proud of—but now, when I walk into that clinic, I’m just a set of risk factors. 

I really wish the people caring for me understood that being "advanced maternal age" doesn't mean I’m just a vessel to be monitored or a statistic to be managed. I’m carrying the memory of a loss that nobody asks about, and I’m terrified in ways that don't show up on a blood panel. 

If there’s one thing I’d want the people in charge of maternity care to know, it’s that compassion shouldn't be the first thing to get cut when the schedule gets tight. I don't need another lecture on folate levels. I need someone to look me in the eye and acknowledge that this is hard, that I am scared, and that I am allowed to be a person, not just a patient. I’m just… I’m tired of performing "perfect." I just want to be seen. That’s all. Thank you for listening. It… it really does help to have said it out loud, even if only to you.
